hypothesis:
Iteration 24 demonstrated genuine OOS alpha (OOS Sharpe 0.83 > IS Sharpe 0.35, WF consistency 1.0, OOS > IS) using a 10/30 SMA crossover per-ETF architecture. The three failures were: (1) IS Sharpe 0.35 far below 1.0, (2) MaxDD -25.1% > -20%, (3) only ~22 trades vs 50+ needed.

Iteration 25 tried to fix all three simultaneously with a regime filter + faster SMA (5/20) — but the 100-day SPY regime filter catastrophically suppressed trade count and introduced IS/OOS divergence (IS 0.49 >> OOS 0.09), destroying the non-overfit property.

This iteration returns strictly to iter 24's architecture (no regime filter, no absolute momentum filter) and makes ONLY TWO targeted changes:

1. **Shorter SMA windows (5/15 instead of 10/30)**: Faster crossovers generate more signals. A 5/15 SMA generates ~2-3x more crossovers than 10/30 on liquid ETFs. With 8 ETFs × ~15 crossovers/year × 5 IS years = ~600 potential trade events. Unlike iter 25 where the regime filter strangled everything, here there is NO filter — all crossovers are taken.

2. **Per-position trailing stop at 12%**: Instead of a portfolio-level regime filter, we implement an EXIT condition: exit any position if it drops 12% from its entry price. This acts as a stop-loss that reduces drawdown during adverse runs (2022 bear market) without suppressing new entries. A 12% trailing stop allows normal fluctuations while preventing catastrophic single-position losses.

Key preservation: NO regime filter (the killer of iters 6, 8, 25), NO absolute momentum filter (the killer of iters 7, 8, 20), NO cross-sectional ranking (the stickiness problem of iters 3-22). Pure independent per-ETF SMA crossover with a simple trailing stop.

The 5/15 SMA generates genuine trend signals — 5-day MA responds to 1-week trends, 15-day to 3-week trends. Crossovers at this frequency capture medium-term momentum cycles (3-6 week trending episodes) that are well-documented in liquid ETFs. Long-only avoids short-side losses.

evaluation.post_mortem: This iteration shows genuinely encouraging signs — particularly the OOS Sharpe (0.72) exceeding IS Sharpe (0.55), a positive IS/OOS relationship suggesting real alpha rather than overfitting, a walk-forward consistency score of 0.846 (above the 0.70 threshold), and OOS MaxDD of only -12.9% well within the -20% limit. The trailing stop appears to be doing its job on the OOS side. Critically, the low overfitting risk is confirmed by OOS > IS Sharpe, consistent with iter 24's healthy dynamic. However, three acceptance criteria remain unmet: (1) IS Sharpe of 0.55 falls well short of the 1.0 target; (2) IS MaxDD of -22.8% narrowly breaches the -20% limit; (3) IS trade count of ~45 falls just short of the 50-trade minimum. The OOS trade count of ~17 is also notably low, suggesting the 5/15 SMA still isn't generating enough crossovers in-sample to satisfy the trade frequency requirement. The strategy is directionally correct — the non-overfit property is preserved and OOS performance is the best seen yet — but it needs more trades and a better IS Sharpe.
evaluation.next_direction: Preserve the no-filter, per-ETF SMA crossover architecture and the 12% trailing stop. The core problem is insufficient IS trade count (~45 vs 50+ needed) and IS Sharpe below 1.0. Consider two targeted changes: (1) Expand the ETF universe from 8 to 12-14 instruments (adding liquid sector ETFs like XLK, XLV, XLE, XLF) to multiply trade opportunities proportionally — more ETFs × same crossover frequency = more trades with no filter added; (2) Slightly tighten the trailing stop from 12% to 8-10% to reduce IS MaxDD below -20% and potentially improve IS Sharpe by cutting losing trades shorter. Do NOT introduce any regime filter or momentum filter. The OOS Sharpe of 0.72 is already at target — the fix must come from improving IS metrics without degrading OOS, which points to universe expansion rather than signal modification.
